description: A loose stone falls from the wall and 'teo' reaches the people below before the explanation does. This immediate warning asks others to notice danger or take care. 'hei' (hey) seeks attention generally; 'teo' says why the attention matters.
articulatory_notes: Set the tongue tip against the backs of the upper front teeth for unvoiced dental 't' and release it into middle-height front 'e'. Keep the syllable boundary as the tongue draws back and the lips round for mid-back 'o'. Stress 'te'.
usage_notes: A separate sentence can name the hazard once the warning has reached its listeners.
